revision.content:
An attempt at building an ebike while spending very little money, but not making everything from scratch or scrap.

== Background ==
Many high-end ebikes (and ebike conversion kits) put motors at the pedals, so the power is transmitted through the chain. These "mid-drive" ebikes can use all the gears the bike has to get good power at a wide range of speeds, and almost always have torque sensors to measure and respond to how hard the rider is pedaling. The sensors, geartrain, compact electronics, and complex mounting mean that this system is about the most expensive kind of ebike. 

Cheaper ebikes put the motor in the hub of the wheel. Some have the motor connected directly to the spokes, making a motor with no moving parts and the ability to spin in both directions (necessary for regenerative braking), but with low torque. Others put a planetary gearbox between the motor and the spokes, almost always with a freewheel. This lets the motor have high torque, at the expense of some complexity and the ability to do regenerative braking.

Those two systems account for the huge majority of commercial ebikes and ebike conversion kits. However, there are at least three systems, available in conversion kits, that are less popular but even cheaper.

Friction-drive systems spin a small motorized wheel directly against the tire. These kits are easily adapted to most bicycles, but are inefficient and accelerate tire wear.

Right-side drive systems put a small motor on the right side of the bicycle, with a chain going to the rear wheel. This may either replace or complement the chain that goes up to the pedals.

Left-side drive systems put the same kind of motor on the left side of the bicycle, and attach an additional sprocket to that side of the wheel, usually by interlacing it into the spokes.


I am trying [https://www.aliexpress.us/item/3256808107651679.html this left-side drive kit], which I bought on 3 Feb 2025 for $68.70 (including shipping and taxes). This is an unusually good price; these kits usually sell for about $90.

== Kit Contents ==
The kit arrived just three days later, in a slightly battered box 10" long x 6¼" wide x 7½" tall and weighing 8.78 pounds. The inside was pretty full of parts; many individually bubble-wrapped but with no padding in the box. The large sprocket had torn a bunch of little holes in the box, but nothing all the way through.

The motor is by far the single heaviest part: 5.31 pounds. Apart from a little brown grease coming out the one-way bearing on its output shaft and two individual styrofoam beads stuck to it, it's clean. The wires are unmarked but the conductors measure about 0.060" diameter, so around 14 or 15 AWG and 2 mm². The insulators are about 0.12" diameter or 3mm, and feel more flexible than PVC. The red insulator is cut all the way through near the motor. The sprocket has 16 teeth.

The wheel sprocket is steel with what looks like a chrome plating. The sprocket is stamped out of steel, and has one 36mm hole in the center and nine slots for mounting. There are sharp burrs on the underside of the hole and slots. It has 32 teeth, so the chain ratio is 2:1.

At the motor's rated speed of 330 RPM, with 20-inch wheels, that works out to 9.8 miles per hour. 29-inch wheels come out to 14.2 miles per hour. Depending on which of my bikes I put this on, I may need to change the sprockets.

To mount the motor, the kit includes a large bracket made from 2.75mm steel and painted black, three 15mm-long M5 button-head bolts, and three washers. You need a 5mm hex key to mount the motor to the bracket. The holes didn't all line up, so I had to turn the middle one into a slot with a chainsaw file. To mount the bracket to the frame, the kit includes four 25mm-long M6 hex-head bolts and two U-brackets with M6 threads swaged into them. You need a 10mm socket, wrench, or nut driver to tighten the bolts. Three of the four M6 slots in the bracket were too tight, and I had to file the paint out from them.
